perturbation.original_text: Producer shall be liable for any improper disclosure of the Confidential Information by its employees.
perturbation.changed_text: Producer shall endeavor to address any improper disclosure of the Confidential Information by its employees.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall be liable' with 'shall endeavor to address' significantly weakens the obligation. 'Endeavor to address' implies an attempt to resolve the issue, but not necessarily liability or responsibility for the disclosure, creating ambiguity.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 3281 states that every person who suffers detriment from the unlawful act or omission of another may recover damages. By changing the clause to 'endeavor to address', the contract obscures the Producer's direct liability for improper disclosures by employees, creating ambiguity about the Writer's right to recover damages for detriment suffered because of the unlawful act or omission of the producer. This weakens the guarantee of compensation that is guaranteed by the law, as endeavoring does not equal full commitment to liability or accountability for the damage.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. COMPENSATORY RELIEF [3281 - 3361] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 1. Damages in General [3281 - 3296] ( Chapter 1 enacted 1872. ) ARTICLE 1. General Principles [3281 - 3283] ( Article 1 enacted 1872. ) 3281. Every person who suffers detriment from the unlawful act or omission of another, may recover from the person in fault a compensation therefor in money, which is called damages. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract text states that the producer 'shall be liable' for improper disclosure, creating a direct obligation. The changed text uses 'shall endeavor to address,' which weakens the obligation and creates ambiguity about the producer's actual liability. California Civil Code § 3281, as presented in Scraped Law Snippet 1, states that every person who suffers detriment from the unlawful act or omission of another may recover damages. The change weakens the Writer's guarantee to recover damages, as endeavoring is less forceful than 'shall be liable' for improper disclosures by employees, making the Producer's accountability unclear.

perturbation.original_text: Writer agrees that it will not contact any party or parties other than Producer concerning the Confidential Information without prior written authorization from Producer during the term of this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Writer agrees to generally refrain from contacting parties other than Producer about the Confidential Information without Producer's consent.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'will not contact any party' with 'agrees to generally refrain from contacting parties' softens the prohibition. Removing 'prior written authorization' removes the requirement of a specific authorization method, making the agreement vague and open to interpretation. 'Generally refrain' does not create a strict prohibition, and 'Producer's consent' lacks the clarity of requiring written authorization, introducing ambiguity.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code § 16600 generally prohibits contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While this provision seems unrelated, the restriction on the Writer contacting other parties can be viewed as a restraint on their ability to network or share their work, especially if the Producer delays or does not follow through. By weakening the strict prohibition ('will not contact') to a general suggestion ('agrees to generally refrain'), the agreement might still be viewed as an attempt to restrain trade, and the ambiguity introduces uncertainty about whether the restraint is reasonable and permissible, creating possible conflict with this code as it is now much more vague and open to interpretation about what is permissible contact and business practice.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the original obligation and introduces ambiguity regarding contacting parties about Confidential Information, potentially conflicting with California Business and Professions Code § 16600. The original text contains a strict prohibition against contacting any party without prior written authorization. The modified text softens this prohibition to 'generally refrain,' making it vague and open to interpretation. 'Generally refrain' does not create a strict prohibition, and 'Producer's consent' lacks the clarity of requiring written authorization. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.' While the original agreement does not directly prohibit the writer from engaging in their profession, the broad interpretation of BPC 16600, along with the softening of the language, raises the possibility of the agreement being viewed as an unreasonable restraint on trade because it has now become more vague and subjective. Therefore, due to the broad interpretation of the law in Snippet 1, the contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: This agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of California without regard to the conflict of laws provisions thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement shall be interpreted under principles generally associated with California law.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be governed by the laws of the State of California without regard to the conflict of laws provisions thereof' to 'shall be interpreted under principles generally associated with California law' introduces a high degree of ambiguity. The original clause clearly indicates that California law applies. The modified clause suggests that only general principles of California law are relevant, leaving open to interpretation which specific laws apply and allowing for arguments that other jurisdictions or legal standards could also be relevant. Removing the exclusion of 'conflict of laws provisions' introduces further uncertainty.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1856, the Parol Evidence Rule, generally states that the terms of a written agreement are considered the final expression of the parties' agreement. By weakening the governing law provision, the modified clause makes it easier to argue that external evidence or principles (not explicitly part of California law) should influence interpretation, potentially contradicting the intent of the Parol Evidence Rule which seeks to uphold the integrity of the written contract. The original clause intended for all California law to govern, while the modification is now more vague and the intent is less clear. The parties could argue the intent of the original contract to introduce ambiguity and potential conflict with the law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1856 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 4. MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [1855 - 2107] ( Heading of Part 4 amended by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) TITLE 1. OF THE GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF EVIDENCE [1855 - 1866] ( Title 1 enacted 1872. ) 1856. (a) Terms set forth in a writing intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to the terms included therein may not be contradicted by evidence of a prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement. (b) The terms set forth in a writing described in subdivision (a) may be explained or supplemented by evidence of consistent additional terms unless the writing is intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement. (c) The terms set forth in a writing described in subdivision (a) may be explained or supplemented by course of dealing or usage of trade or by course of performance. (d) The court shall determine whether the writing is intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to the terms included therein and whether the writing is intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement. (e) Where a mistake or imperfection of the writing is put in issue by the pleadings, this section does not exclude evidence relevant to that issue. (f
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the governing law provision, making it easier to argue that external evidence or principles (not explicitly part of California law) should influence interpretation. This contradicts California Code of Civil Procedure § 1856, which aims to uphold the integrity of the written contract by preventing its terms from being contradicted by prior or contemporaneous agreements, as stated in snippet 1: "Terms set forth in a writing intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to the terms included therein may not be contradicted by evidence of a prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement.". The original clause intended for all California law to govern, while the modification is more vague, creating ambiguity and a potential contradiction.
